author: śrīla saccidānanda bhaktibinoda ṭhākura
verses:
kṛṣṇavaṁśīgīta śuni’, dekhi’ citrapaṭakhāni,
lokamukhe guṇa śravaṇiyā
pūrvarāgākrānta cita, unmāda⋅lakṣaṇānvita,
sakhīsaṅge calilā dhāiyā

nikuñjakānane karila abhisāra
nā mānila nivāraṇa, gṛhakārya agaṇana,
dharmādharma nā karila vicāra

jamunāpuline giyā, sakhīgaṇe sambodhiyā,
jijñāsila priyera uddeśa
chāḍila prāṇera bhaya, vanete praveśa haya,
vaṁśīdhvani kariyā nirdeśa

nadī jathā sindhu⋅prati, dhāya ati vegavatī,
seirūpa rasavatī satī
ati vege kuñjavane, giyā kṛṣṇa⋅sannidhāne,
ātmanivedane kaila mati

kena mora durbalā lekhanī nāhi sare
abhisāra āramtiyā sakampa antare

milana, sambhoga, vipralambhādi⋅varṇana
prakāśa karite nāhi sare mama mana

durbhāgā nā bujhe rāsalīlā⋅tattvasāra
śūkara jemana nāhi cine muktāhāra

adhikārahīna⋅jana⋅maṅgala cintiyā
kīrtana karinu śeṣa, kāla vicāriyā
en_translation:
Hearing Kṛṣṇa's flute song, seeing His beautiful form in a painting, and hearing of His qualities, her heart was afflicted by initial attraction; showing symptoms of madness, she ran swiftly with her companions.

She made her tryst toward the forest bower. She heeded no prevention, nor her countless household duties, nor did she consider right or wrong.

Going to the bank of the Yamunā and addressing her companions, she inquired about the whereabouts of her beloved. Abandoning all fear for her life, she entered the forest, guided by the sound of the flute.

Just as a river swiftly rushes toward the ocean, that chaste woman, full of mellows, went with great speed to the forest grove near Kṛṣṇa; with a mood of self-surrender, she gave herself to Him.

Why does my feeble pen not move, as I begin to describe the tryst with a trembling heart?

My mind does not move to reveal the meeting, the enjoyment, the separation, and other such topics.

An unfortunate person cannot understand the essence of the divine rāsa dance, just as a hog cannot recognize a pearl necklace.

Thinking of the welfare of those who are not yet qualified, I conclude this narration here, considering the time.
